text: Under general anesthesia, the patient is positioned in a frog-like posture. An inframilical incision is made, and a 5mm trocar is inserted using the Hasson technique. Two additional 3mm trocars are inserted under direct vision in the right flank and left hypochondrium.
The surgeon performs an extracorporeal stitch to elevate the round ligament, followed by a similar stitch to suspend the right hepatic lobe.

The surgeon identifies a distended duodenum and transects peritoneal bands between the liver, retroperitoneum, and bowel. The superior mesenteric artery is visible. After retracting and lifting the duodenum, a duodenal volvulus is revealed along an edematous mesenteric stalk. The cecum and appendix are observed in a high and medial position below the liver, and the volvulus is undone.

The surgeon identifies an internal para duodenal hernia and carefully dissects and removes the hernia sac. Following this, an appendectomy is performed. The appendicular artery is skeletonized and cauterized, and the appendix is exteriorized through the umbilical incision for extracorporeal completion of the appendectomy.
The surgeon returns the cecum to the abdominal cavity and proceeds to widen the mesentery, ensuring proper anatomical positioning.
The surgeon confirms the correct anatomical positioning of the small bowel on the right side and the colon on the left side within the abdominal cavity.
